**Eli Herschel Wallach** ([/ˈiːlaɪ ˈwɒlək/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*EE-ly WOL-ək*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); December 7, 1915 – June 24, 2014) was an American film, television, and stage actor from New York City. Known for his [character actor](/wiki/Character_actor "Character actor") roles, his entertainment career spanned over six decades. He received a [BAFTA Award](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Awards "British Academy Film Awards"), a [Tony Award](/wiki/Tony_Awards "Tony Awards"), and a [Primetime Emmy Award](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Awards "Primetime Emmy Awards"). He also was inducted into the [American Theater Hall of Fame](/wiki/American_Theater_Hall_of_Fame "American Theater Hall of Fame") in 1988 and received the [Academy Honorary Award](/wiki/Academy_Honorary_Award "Academy Honorary Award") in 2010.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Originally trained in stage acting, he garnered over 90 film credits. He and his wife [Anne Jackson](/wiki/Anne_Jackson "Anne Jackson") often appeared together on stage, eventually becoming a notable acting couple in American theater. Wallach initially studied [method acting](/wiki/Method_acting "Method acting") under [Sanford Meisner](/wiki/Sanford_Meisner "Sanford Meisner") and later became a founding member of the [Actors Studio](/wiki/Actors_Studio "Actors Studio"), where he studied under [Lee Strasberg](/wiki/Lee_Strasberg "Lee Strasberg"). He played a wide variety of roles throughout his career, primarily as a [supporting actor](/wiki/Supporting_actor "Supporting actor"). He won the [Tony Award for Best Supporting or Featured Actor in a Play](/wiki/Tony_Award_for_Best_Featured_Actor_in_a_Play "Tony Award for Best Featured Actor in a Play") for *[The Rose Tattoo](/wiki/The_Rose_Tattoo "The Rose Tattoo")* (1951).

For his debut screen performance in *[Baby Doll](/wiki/Baby_Doll "Baby Doll")* (1956), he won a [BAFTA Award for Best Newcomer](/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Most_Promising_Newcomer_to_Leading_Film_Roles "BAFTA Award for Most Promising Newcomer to Leading Film Roles") and a [Golden Globe Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards") nomination. Among his other most famous roles are Calvera in *[The Magnificent Seven](/wiki/The_Magnificent_Seven "The Magnificent Seven")* (1960), Guido in *[The Misfits](/wiki/The_Misfits_(1961_film) "The Misfits (1961 film)")* (1961), Tuco ("The Ugly") in *[The Good, the Bad and the Ugly](/wiki/The_Good,_the_Bad_and_the_Ugly "The Good, the Bad and the Ugly")* (1966) and [Don Altobello](/wiki/Don_Altobello "Don Altobello") in *[The Godfather Part III](/wiki/The_Godfather_Part_III "The Godfather Part III")* (1990). Other notable films include *[How the West Was Won](/wiki/How_the_West_Was_Won_(film) "How the West Was Won (film)")* (1962), *[Tough Guys](/wiki/Tough_Guys "Tough Guys")* (1986), *[The Two Jakes](/wiki/The_Two_Jakes "The Two Jakes")* (1990), *[The Associate](/wiki/The_Associate_(1996_film) "The Associate (1996 film)")* (1996), *[The Holiday](/wiki/The_Holiday "The Holiday")* (2006), *[Wall Street: Money Never Sleeps](/wiki/Wall_Street:_Money_Never_Sleeps "Wall Street: Money Never Sleeps")*, and *[The Ghost Writer](/wiki/The_Ghost_Writer_(film) "The Ghost Writer (film)")* (both 2010). He received [Primetime Emmy Award](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Awards "Primetime Emmy Awards") nominations for *[Studio 60 on the Sunset Strip](/wiki/Studio_60_on_the_Sunset_Strip "Studio 60 on the Sunset Strip")* (2007) and *[Nurse Jackie](/wiki/Nurse_Jackie "Nurse Jackie")* (2011).

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eli_Wallach&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Eli Herschel Wallach ([Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language"): עלי הערשל וואלאך) was born on December 7, 1915, at 156 Union Street in [Red Hook, Brooklyn](/wiki/Red_Hook,_Brooklyn "Red Hook, Brooklyn"), a son of [Polish Jewish](/wiki/Polish_Jewish "Polish Jewish") immigrants Abraham ([Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language"): אברהם וואלאך) and Bertha Wallach (née Schorr; [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language"): בערטה שורר וואלאך) from [Przemyśl](/wiki/Przemy%C5%9Bl "Przemyśl"). He had a brother and two sisters.[[3]](#cite_note-Ross-3) He and his family were among only a few Jews in an otherwise Italian American neighborhood.[[4]](#cite_note-forward-4)[[5]](#cite_note-film_reference-5) His parents owned Bertha's Candy Store.[[3]](#cite_note-Ross-3) Wallach graduated in 1936 from the [University of Texas](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin") with a degree in history.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In a later interview, Wallach said that he learned to ride horses while in Texas, explaining that he liked Texas because "It opened my eyes to the word *friendship...* You could rely on people. If they gave you their word, that was it... It was an education."[[7]](#cite_note-Texas-7)

Two years later he earned a master of arts degree in education from the [City College of New York](/wiki/City_College_of_New_York "City College of New York").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) He gained his first method acting experience at the [Neighborhood Playhouse School of the Theatre](/wiki/Neighborhood_Playhouse_School_of_the_Theatre "Neighborhood Playhouse School of the Theatre") in New York City, where he studied under [Sanford Meisner](/wiki/Sanford_Meisner "Sanford Meisner").[[10]](#cite_note-10) There, according to Wallach, actors were forced to "unlearn" all their physical and vocal mannerisms, while traditional stage etiquette and "singsong" deliveries were "utterly excised" from his classroom.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Military service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eli_Wallach&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Military service")]

Wallach's education was cut short when he was drafted[[3]](#cite_note-Ross-3)[[12]](#cite_note-12) into the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") in 1940.[[13]](#cite_note-we_served-13)[[14]](#cite_note-vetbiz-14) He served as a staff sergeant and [medic](/wiki/Combat_medic "Combat medic")[[15]](#cite_note-herald-15) in a military hospital in Hawaii and later was sent to [Officer Candidate School](/wiki/Officer_Candidate_School "Officer Candidate School") (OCS) in [Abilene, Texas](/wiki/Abilene,_Texas "Abilene, Texas"), to train as a medical administrative officer.[[13]](#cite_note-we_served-13)[[15]](#cite_note-herald-15)[[16]](#cite_note-britannica-16)[[17]](#cite_note-nytobit-17) Commissioned as a [second lieutenant](/wiki/Second_lieutenant "Second lieutenant"), he was ordered to [Casablanca](/wiki/Casablanca "Casablanca"). Later, when he was serving in France, a senior officer noticed his acting career and asked him to create a show for the patients. He and his unit wrote a play called *Is This the Army?*, which was inspired by Irving Berlin's *[This Is the Army](/wiki/This_Is_the_Army "This Is the Army")*. In the comedy, Wallach and the other actors mocked Axis dictators, with Wallach portraying [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler").[[18]](#cite_note-WallachSPBio-18) Wallach was discharged as a [captain](/wiki/Captain_(United_States_O-3) "Captain (United States O-3)") following the war's end in 1945.[[3]](#cite_note-Ross-3)[[13]](#cite_note-we_served-13)[[17]](#cite_note-nytobit-17) He was awarded the [Army Good Conduct Medal](/wiki/Good_Conduct_Medal_(United_States) "Good Conduct Medal (United States)"), the [American Defense Service Medal](/wiki/American_Defense_Service_Medal "American Defense Service Medal"), the [American Campaign Medal](/wiki/American_Campaign_Medal "American Campaign Medal"), the [Asiatic–Pacific Campaign Medal](/wiki/Asiatic%E2%80%93Pacific_Campaign_Medal "Asiatic–Pacific Campaign Medal"), the [European–African–Middle Eastern Campaign Medal](/wiki/European%E2%80%93African%E2%80%93Middle_Eastern_Campaign_Medal "European–African–Middle Eastern Campaign Medal") and the [World War II Victory Medal](/wiki/World_War_II_Victory_Medal_(United_States) "World War II Victory Medal (United States)").[[13]](#cite_note-we_served-13)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eli_Wallach&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Career")]

Main article: [Eli Wallach credits](/wiki/Eli_Wallach_credits "Eli Wallach credits")

### Stage actor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eli_Wallach&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Stage actor")]

Wallach took classes in acting at the [Dramatic Workshop](/wiki/Dramatic_Workshop "Dramatic Workshop") of [the New School](/wiki/The_New_School "The New School") in New York City with the influential German director [Erwin Piscator](/wiki/Erwin_Piscator "Erwin Piscator"). He later became a founding member of the [Actors Studio](/wiki/Actors_Studio "Actors Studio"), taught by [Lee Strasberg](/wiki/Lee_Strasberg "Lee Strasberg"). There, he studied more method acting technique with founding member [Robert Lewis](/wiki/Robert_Lewis_(director) "Robert Lewis (director)"), and with other students including [Marlon Brando](/wiki/Marlon_Brando "Marlon Brando"), [Montgomery Clift](/wiki/Montgomery_Clift "Montgomery Clift"), [Herbert Berghof](/wiki/Herbert_Berghof "Herbert Berghof"), [Sidney Lumet](/wiki/Sidney_Lumet "Sidney Lumet"), and his soon-to-be wife, [Anne Jackson](/wiki/Anne_Jackson "Anne Jackson").[[19]](#cite_note-19) Wallach became [Marilyn Monroe](/wiki/Marilyn_Monroe "Marilyn Monroe")'s first new friend when she became a student at the Actors Studio, once insisting on watching him perform in *[The Teahouse of the August Moon](/wiki/The_Teahouse_of_the_August_Moon_(play) "The Teahouse of the August Moon (play)")* from the backstage wings, simply to see up close how experienced actors perform a two-hour play.[[20]](#cite_note-Gottfried-20) She also became friends with his wife, Anne Jackson, also studying at the Studio, and would visit the couple at their home and sometimes babysit their new child.[[21]](#cite_note-Harding-21)

In 1945 Wallach made his [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") debut and he won a Tony Award in 1951 for his performance alongside [Maureen Stapleton](/wiki/Maureen_Stapleton "Maureen Stapleton") in the [Tennessee Williams](/wiki/Tennessee_Williams "Tennessee Williams") play *[The Rose Tattoo](/wiki/The_Rose_Tattoo "The Rose Tattoo")*.[[22]](#cite_note-playbill1-22) His other theater credits include *[Mister Roberts](/wiki/Mister_Roberts_(play) "Mister Roberts (play)")*, *[The Teahouse of the August Moon](/wiki/The_Teahouse_of_the_August_Moon_(play) "The Teahouse of the August Moon (play)")*, *[Camino Real](/wiki/Camino_Real_(play) "Camino Real (play)")*, *[Major Barbara](/wiki/Major_Barbara "Major Barbara")* (in which director [Charles Laughton](/wiki/Charles_Laughton "Charles Laughton") discouraged Wallach's established method acting style),[[22]](#cite_note-playbill1-22) *[Luv](/wiki/Luv_(play) "Luv (play)")*, and *[Staircase](/wiki/Staircase_(play) "Staircase (play)")*, co-starring [Milo O'Shea](/wiki/Milo_O%27Shea "Milo O'Shea"), which was a serious depiction of an aging homosexual couple. He also played a role in a tour of *[Antony and Cleopatra](/wiki/Antony_and_Cleopatra "Antony and Cleopatra"),* produced by the actress [Katharine Cornell](/wiki/Katharine_Cornell "Katharine Cornell") in 1946.[[23]](#cite_note-23) He exposed Americans to the work of playwright [Eugène Ionesco](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Ionesco "Eugène Ionesco") in plays including *[The Chairs](/wiki/The_Chairs "The Chairs")* and *[The Lesson](/wiki/The_Lesson "The Lesson")* in 1958, and in 1961 *[Rhinoceros](/wiki/Rhinoceros_(play) "Rhinoceros (play)")* opposite [Zero Mostel](/wiki/Zero_Mostel "Zero Mostel").[[22]](#cite_note-playbill1-22) He last starred on stage as the title character in *[Visiting Mr. Green](/wiki/Visiting_Mr._Green "Visiting Mr. Green")*.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/7a/Eli_Wallach_and_Maureen_Stapleton_-_1951.jpg/250px-Eli_Wallach_and_Maureen_Stapleton_-_1951.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eli_Wallach_and_Maureen_Stapleton_-_1951.jpg)

With [Maureen Stapleton](/wiki/Maureen_Stapleton "Maureen Stapleton") in *The Rose Tattoo* (1951)

The stage was where Wallach focused his early career. From 1945 to 1950 he and his wife, [Anne Jackson](/wiki/Anne_Jackson "Anne Jackson"), worked together acting in various plays by [Tennessee Williams](/wiki/Tennessee_Williams "Tennessee Williams"). The five years following, he continued only working on stage, not becoming involved in film work until 1956. During those years, however, they were generally having a hard time making ends meet. He recalls they were getting along on unemployment insurance and living in a one-room, $35 a month apartment on lower Fifth Avenue in the Village.[[3]](#cite_note-Ross-3) When he did get offered early movie parts, he turned them down with no regrets, and very early in his career he explained his reasoning:

> What do I need a movie for? The stage is on a higher level in every way, and a more satisfying medium. Movies, by comparison, are like calendar art next to great paintings. You can't really do very much in movies or in television, but the stage is such an anarchistic medium.[[3]](#cite_note-Ross-3)

He said that the stage was what attracted him most and what he "needed" to do.[[25]](#cite_note-25) "Acting is the most alive thing I can do, and the most joyous", he stated.[[3]](#cite_note-Ross-3)

Wallach and Jackson became one of the best-known acting couples in the American theater, as iconic as Alfred Lunt and Lynn Fontanne, and Jessica Tandy and Hume Cronyn,[[22]](#cite_note-playbill1-22) and they looked for opportunities to work together. During an interview, he said of Jackson, "I have tremendous respect and admiration for her as an actress . . . we have a terrific working compatibility when we're in the same play, especially when the play means something important to us."[[3]](#cite_note-Ross-3): 159–160 When he did gravitate toward accepting parts in films, he did so to "help pay the bills", he said, adding, "for actors, movies are a means to an end."[[26]](#cite_note-NYTobit-26)

Despite the fact that he eventually acted in over 90 films and almost as many television dramas,[[27]](#cite_note-NYT-Scott-27) he continued to accept stage parts throughout his career, often with Jackson. They played in comedies like *The Typists* and *The Tiger* in 1963, and acted together in *Waltz of the Toreadors* in 1973. In 1978 they played in a revival of *The Diary of Anne Frank*, along with their daughters, and in 1984 they acted in *Nest of the Wood Grouse,* directed by [Joseph Papp](/wiki/Joseph_Papp "Joseph Papp"). Four years later, in 1988, they acted in a revival of *Cafe Crown*, a portrait of the [Yiddish theater](/wiki/Yiddish_theater "Yiddish theater") scene during its prime.[[26]](#cite_note-NYTobit-26) They continued acting together as late as 2000, while he also took on roles alone throughout all those years.[[26]](#cite_note-NYTobit-26)

### Film and television roles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eli_Wallach&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Film and television roles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/53/Baby_Doll_-1.jpg/330px-Baby_Doll_-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baby_Doll_-1.jpg)

Wallach and [Carroll Baker](/wiki/Carroll_Baker "Carroll Baker") in the swing scene from *Baby Doll*

Wallach's film debut was in [Elia Kazan](/wiki/Elia_Kazan "Elia Kazan")'s controversial 1956 *[Baby Doll](/wiki/Baby_Doll "Baby Doll")*, for which he won the [British Academy Film Awards](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Awards "British Academy Film Awards") (BAFTA) as "Most Promising Newcomer."[[28]](#cite_note-28) *Baby Doll* was controversial because of its underlying sexual theme. Director [Elia Kazan](/wiki/Elia_Kazan "Elia Kazan") however, set explicit limits on Wallach's scenes, telling him not to actually seduce [Carroll Baker](/wiki/Carroll_Baker "Carroll Baker"), but instead to create an unfulfilled erotic tension.[[29]](#cite_note-Wallach-29) Kazan later explained his reasoning:

> What is erotic about sex to me is the seduction, not the act ... The scene on the swing with Eli Wallach and Carroll Baker in *Baby Doll* is my exact idea of what eroticism in films should be.[[30]](#cite_note-Young-30)

Wallach went on to a prolific career as "one of the greatest '[character actors](/wiki/Character_actor "Character actor")' ever to appear on stage and screen", notes [Turner Classic Movies](/wiki/Turner_Classic_Movies "Turner Classic Movies"),[[31]](#cite_note-TCM-31) acting in over 90 films.[[27]](#cite_note-NYT-Scott-27) Having grown up on the "mean streets" of an Italian American neighborhood,[[32]](#cite_note-McNulty-32) and his versatility as a method actor, Wallach developed the ability to play a wide variety of different roles, although he tried not to get pinned down to any single type of character. "Right now I'm playing an old man", he said at age 84. But "I've been through all the ethnic groups, from Mexican bandits to Italian Mafia heads to Okinawans to half-breeds, and now I'm playing old Jews. Who knows?"[[7]](#cite_note-Texas-7)

Noting this versatility as a character actor, the [Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Academy_of_Motion_Picture_Arts_and_Sciences "Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences") called him "the quintessential chameleon", with the ability to play different characters "effortlessly",[[33]](#cite_note-Mirror-33) and [*L.A. Times*](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times") theater critic [Charles McNulty](/wiki/Charles_McNulty "Charles McNulty") saw Wallach's "power to illuminate" his various screen or stage [personas](/wiki/Persona "Persona") as being "radioactive."[[32]](#cite_note-McNulty-32) *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* has written that "Wallach was made for character acting", and includes movie clips from some of his most memorable roles in a tribute to him.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

In 1961, Wallach co-starred with [Marilyn Monroe](/wiki/Marilyn_Monroe "Marilyn Monroe"), [Montgomery Clift](/wiki/Montgomery_Clift "Montgomery Clift") and [Clark Gable](/wiki/Clark_Gable "Clark Gable") in *[The Misfits](/wiki/The_Misfits_(1961_film) "The Misfits (1961 film)")*, Monroe's and Gable's last film before their deaths.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) Wallach never learned why he was cast in the film, although he suspected that Monroe had something to do with it.[[21]](#cite_note-Harding-21) Its screenwriter, [Arthur Miller](/wiki/Arthur_Miller "Arthur Miller"), who was married to Monroe at the time, said that "Eli Wallach is the happiest good actor I've ever known. He really enjoys the work."[[31]](#cite_note-TCM-31)

Some of his other films included *[The Lineup](/wiki/The_Lineup_(film) "The Lineup (film)")* (1958); *[Lord Jim](/wiki/Lord_Jim_(1965_film) "Lord Jim (1965 film)")* (1965) with [Peter O'Toole](/wiki/Peter_O%27Toole "Peter O'Toole"); a comic role in *[How to Steal a Million](/wiki/How_to_Steal_a_Million "How to Steal a Million")* (1966), again with O'Toole, and [Audrey Hepburn](/wiki/Audrey_Hepburn "Audrey Hepburn"); and as Tuco ("the Ugly") in [Sergio Leone](/wiki/Sergio_Leone "Sergio Leone")'s *[The Good, the Bad and the Ugly](/wiki/The_Good,_the_Bad_and_the_Ugly "The Good, the Bad and the Ugly")* (1966) with [Clint Eastwood](/wiki/Clint_Eastwood "Clint Eastwood") and [Lee Van Cleef](/wiki/Lee_Van_Cleef "Lee Van Cleef"), followed by other [Spaghetti Westerns](/wiki/Spaghetti_Western "Spaghetti Western"), such as *[Ace High](/wiki/Ace_High_(1968_film) "Ace High (1968 film)")*. At one point, [Henry Fonda](/wiki/Henry_Fonda "Henry Fonda") had asked Wallach whether he himself should accept a part offered to him to act in a similar Western, *[Once Upon a Time in the West](/wiki/Once_Upon_a_Time_in_the_West "Once Upon a Time in the West")* (1968), which would also be directed by Leone. Wallach said "Yes, you'll enjoy the challenge", and Fonda later thanked Wallach for that advice.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Wallach and Eastwood became friends during the filming of *The Good, the Bad and the Ugly* and he recalled their off-work time together: "Clint was the tall, silent type. He's the kind where you open up and do all the talking. He smiles and nods and stores it all away in that wonderful calculator of a brain."[[38]](#cite_note-38) In 2003 Wallach acted in [*Mystic River*](/wiki/Mystic_River_(film) "Mystic River (film)"), produced and directed by Eastwood, who once said "working with Eli Wallach has been one of the great pleasures of my life."[[31]](#cite_note-TCM-31)

A pivotal moment in Wallach's career came in 1953, when he, along with [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra") and [Harvey Lembeck](/wiki/Harvey_Lembeck "Harvey Lembeck"), tried out for the role of Maggio in the film *[From Here to Eternity](/wiki/From_Here_to_Eternity "From Here to Eternity")*. Sinatra biographer Kitty Kelly notes that while Sinatra's test was good, it had none of the "consummate acting ability" of Wallach. Producer [Harry Cohn](/wiki/Harry_Cohn "Harry Cohn") and director [Fred Zinnemann](/wiki/Fred_Zinnemann "Fred Zinnemann") were "dazzled" by Wallach's screen test and wanted him to play the part. However, Wallach had previously been offered an important role in another [Tennessee Williams](/wiki/Tennessee_Williams "Tennessee Williams") play, *[Camino Real](/wiki/Camino_Real_(play) "Camino Real (play)")*, to be directed by [Elia Kazan](/wiki/Elia_Kazan "Elia Kazan"), and turned down the movie role. Wallach said that when he learned that the play had finally received financing, he "grabbed" the opportunity: "It was a remarkable piece of writing by the leading playwright in America and it was going to be directed by the country's best. There really wasn't much of a choice for me."[[39]](#cite_note-39) The film, however, went on to win eight Academy Awards, including one for Sinatra, which revived his career. Wallach recalled afterwards, "Whenever Sinatra saw me, he'd say, 'Hello, you crazy actor!'"[[4]](#cite_note-forward-4) Wallach, however, said he had no regrets.

Film historian James Welsh states that during Wallach's career, he appeared in most of the "prestige" television dramas during the "Golden Age" of the 1950s, including *Studio One*, *The Philco Television Playhouse*, *The Armstrong Circle Theatre*, *Playhouse 90*, and *The Hallmark Hall of Fame*, among others. He won the 1966–1967 Emmy Award for his role in the telefilm *The Poppy is Also a Flower*.[[40]](#cite_note-filmreference-40)[[41]](#cite_note-Welsh-41) In 2006 Wallach appeared on [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC")'s *[Studio 60 on the Sunset Strip](/wiki/Studio_60_on_the_Sunset_Strip "Studio 60 on the Sunset Strip")*, playing a former writer who was [blacklisted](/wiki/Hollywood_blacklist "Hollywood blacklist") in the 1950s. His character was a writer on *The Philco Comedy Hour*, a show that aired on a fictional NBS network. This is a reference to *[The Philco Television Playhouse](/wiki/The_Philco_Television_Playhouse "The Philco Television Playhouse")*, in several episodes of which Wallach actually appeared in 1955. Wallach earned a [2007 Emmy](/wiki/59th_Primetime_Emmy_Awards "59th Primetime Emmy Awards") nomination for his work on the show.[[42]](#cite_note-TVWeek2007Emmys-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Eli_Wallach_2010_TCM_Classic_Film_Festival_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Eli_Wallach_2010_TCM_Classic_Film_Festival_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eli_Wallach_2010_TCM_Classic_Film_Festival_(cropped).jpg)

Wallach at the 2010 TCM Classic Film Festival

During the filming of *The Good, the Bad and the Ugly*, Wallach nearly died three times. Once, he accidentally drank a bottle of acid which was placed next to his pop bottle; another time was in a scene where he was about to be hanged, someone fired a pistol which caused the horse underneath him to bolt and run while Wallach's hands were still tied behind his back; in a different scene with him lying on a railroad track, he was close to being decapitated by steps jutting out from the train.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Wallach appeared as [DC Comics](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics")' supervillain [Mr. Freeze](/wiki/Mr._Freeze "Mr. Freeze") in the 1960s *[Batman](/wiki/Batman_(TV_series) "Batman (TV series)")* television series. He said that he received more fan mail about his role as Mr. Freeze than for all his other roles combined.[[44]](#cite_note-ReutersObit-44) He played Gus Farber in the television miniseries *[Seventh Avenue](/wiki/Seventh_Avenue_(miniseries) "Seventh Avenue (miniseries)")* in 1977, and 10 years later, at the age of 71, he starred alongside Michael Landon in *[Highway to Heaven](/wiki/Highway_to_Heaven "Highway to Heaven")* episode "A Father's Faith". Three years later, he played aging [mob boss](/wiki/Mob_boss "Mob boss") Don Altobello in [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola")'s *The Godfather Part III*. Eli Wallach was an actor who guest-starred on Law & Order in the episode "The Working Stiff" as Simon Vilanis in 1991.

On November 13, 2010, at the age of 94, Wallach received an [Academy Honorary Award](/wiki/Academy_Honorary_Award "Academy Honorary Award") for his contribution to the film industry from the [Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Academy_of_Motion_Picture_Arts_and_Sciences "Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences").[[45]](#cite_note-45) A few years prior to that event, [Kate Winslet](/wiki/Kate_Winslet "Kate Winslet") told another audience that Wallach, with whom she acted in *[The Holiday](/wiki/The_Holiday "The Holiday")* in 2006,[[46]](#cite_note-46) was one of the "most charismatic men" she'd met, and her "very own sexiest man alive."[[33]](#cite_note-Mirror-33)

Wallach's final performance was in the short film *The Train* (2015). Wallach plays a [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust") survivor who, in a meeting, teaches a self-consumed and preoccupied young man that life can change in a moment. The short was shot in early 2014 and premiered on August 6, 2015, at the [Rhode Island International Film Festival](/wiki/Rhode_Island_International_Film_Festival "Rhode Island International Film Festival").[[47]](#cite_note-47)

Between 1984 and 1997, he also performed voiceovers in a series of television commercials for the [Toyota Pickup](/wiki/Toyota_Pickup "Toyota Pickup").

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eli_Wallach&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Wallach was married to actress [Anne Jackson](/wiki/Anne_Jackson "Anne Jackson"), with whom he frequently shared the screen, from March 5, 1948, until his death on June 24, 2014. They had three children: Peter, Katherine and Roberta.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

In 2008, Wallach lost sight in his left eye due to a stroke.[[33]](#cite_note-Mirror-33)

His niece is historian [Joan Wallach Scott](/wiki/Joan_Wallach_Scott "Joan Wallach Scott") (daughter of his brother Sam). [A.O. Scott](/wiki/A.O._Scott "A.O. Scott"), a film critic for *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, is his great-nephew.[[27]](#cite_note-NYT-Scott-27)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eli_Wallach&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Death")]

Wallach died on June 24, 2014, of [natural causes](/wiki/Natural_causes "Natural causes") at the age of 98. His body was cremated.[[26]](#cite_note-NYTobit-26)[[44]](#cite_note-ReutersObit-44)

Katherine Wallach told *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* that Anne Jackson died on April 12, 2016, aged 90, at her home in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan").[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)
